REVIEW_COMPLETE

---

## Security Review Summary — 2026-02-23

**5 findings total: 0 critical, 0 high, 1 medium, 4 low.**

Reports written to:
- `reports/2026-02-23-security-review.md`
- `reports/2026-02-23-security-review.json`

---

### Findings

| ID | Severity | Title |
|----|----------|-------|
| `dos-sec-2026-02-23-001` | **Medium** | Health tracker names exposed in push notification titles on lock screen |
| `dos-sec-2026-02-23-002` | Low | Apple Connect API key ID + issuer ID committed in `eas.json` |
| `dos-sec-2026-02-23-003` | Low | Sentry lacks `beforeSend` hook to scrub health data from error reports |
| `dos-sec-2026-02-23-004` | Low | Share code rate limiting uses first `x-forwarded-for` entry (spoofable via proxy) |
| `dos-sec-2026-02-23-005` | Low | Offline dose queue silently falls back to cleartext AsyncStorage when SecureStore unavailable |

---

### Key Finding Detail

**Medium — tracker names on lock screen (`notifications.ts:919,975`):** Medication reminder titles correctly say `"Emma: Time for a dose"` (no med name), but health tracker reminders expose the user-defined `display_name` in the title: `"Emma: Time to log Blood Pressure"`. This is visible on the lock screen to anyone who sees the device. Fix: use `"Time to log"` as the generic title and move the tracker name to the notification body (hidden by iOS when "Show Previews" is off).

---

### Overall Posture: Strong

The codebase shows mature security practices — CSPRNG invite codes with expiration, full Supabase RLS across all 13 tables, SecureStore for auth tokens, generic titles on medication notifications, email reminders with zero PHI, database-backed rate limiting that fails closed, and atomic cascade deletes with authorization checks.
